- From II Year to III Year: A student should have cleared all the Course Work requirements of I Year and passed at least 24 credits in 3rd Semester, 4th Semester, and make-up examinations put together.

incorrect.question: What is the process for submitting and evaluating the M.Tech dissertation (Part-B) at NIT Warangal?
incorrect.bot_answer:
The process for submitting and evaluating the M.Tech dissertation (Part-B) at NIT Warangal involves several steps. Students are required to submit a report on the dissertation work carried out by them, using a template that can be downloaded from the NIT Warangal website. The report should show that the plagiarism is within 20% along with three annexures as stipulated in the Anti-plagiarism policy. 

The evaluation of Dissertation Part-B is done for 100 marks, with the following weightages: 
* Periodic evaluation by Guide: 40 marks
* Midterm review: 20 marks
* End Semester viva-voce examination: 40 marks

The midterm review and the end semester viva-voce examination are conducted by a committee constituted by the Head of the Department. The student is required to submit three/four bound copies along with a soft copy of the dissertation to the Head of the Department/Centre within the last date prescribed in the Academic Calendar. 

If the performance of the student in Dissertation Part-B is not satisfactory, they will be awarded a 'U' grade and will have to repeat the dissertation work by registering in the next semester. They may be given an option to either continue with the same topic and guide or change the guide and topic of their dissertation.

(Source: Academic-Regulations-M.Tech-2024-2025_2024-7-12-10-47-33)

incorrect.question: What steps must a B.Tech student follow to appeal against an erroneous grade after result publication?
incorrect.bot_answer:
To appeal against an erroneous grade after result publication, a B.Tech student must follow these steps:

incorrect.question: What is the step-by-step process for submitting a PhD thesis at NIT Warangal, from the pre-synopsis seminar to the award of degree?
incorrect.bot_answer:
The step-by-step process for submitting a PhD thesis at NIT Warangal is as follows:

1. Pre-synopsis seminar: The scholar is required to present a pre-synopsis seminar to review their progress, at least one seminar per semester before the Doctoral Scrutiny Committee (DSC).
2. Submission of synopsis: The scholar submits their synopsis along with the required certificates, including a declaration by the Research Supervisor in a prescribed proforma (Ph.D. THESIS SUBMISSION CERTIFICATE).
3. Preparation of thesis: The scholar prepares their thesis, ensuring that it does not contain any work previously submitted for the award of any degree, and obtains an originality report generated by any plagiarism checking software suggested by the Institute.
4. Submission of thesis: The scholar submits their thesis, accompanied by the originality report and three annexures, to the Institute.
5. Evaluation by examiners: The thesis is evaluated by examiners, who can recommend that the thesis be accepted for the award of the PhD degree, subject to clarification of certain points at the time of viva-voce, or subject to minor modifications and correction of errors.
6. Viva-voce: The scholar presents their thesis in a viva-voce examination, where they clarify any points raised by the examiners.
7. Award of degree: If the thesis is accepted, the scholar is awarded the PhD degree.

Note: The exact timeline and specific requirements may vary, but the above steps provide a general outline of the process.

(Source: PhD_Regulations_(2024-25)_2024-7-17-11-38-22)

incorrect.question: What is the complete two-part process of the Comprehensive Examination for PhD scholars at NIT Warangal, including committee composition and timing constraints?
incorrect.bot_answer:
The Comprehensive Examination for PhD scholars at NIT Warangal is a two-part process. Part-1 consists of a written test in a subject where the syllabus is comprehensively framed by the DSC members, including all topics relevant to the PhD, methods, and tools. The student must score at least 50 percent in this written exam to proceed to Part-2. Part-2 involves a seminar presentation by the scholar on the state of the art in the proposed research area, along with objectives and methodology, to the Comprehensive Examination Committee. The committee consists of DSC members and two other faculty members, either Associate Professor or Professor level, nominated by the Chairman, DSC, with one member from an allied department. The Comprehensive Examination must be completed within 1½ years, with a maximum of two attempts, and there must be a minimum gap of one month between two successive attempts.

incorrect.question: What is the process when both external examiners reject a PhD thesis at NIT Warangal?

The resubmission of the thesis without modification along the lines of criticism made by the earlier examiners will not be allowed.

incorrect.question: In the B.Tech CSE curriculum, what courses form the 7th semester and how does it prepare students for the 8th semester?
incorrect.bot_answer:
The 7th semester in the B.Tech CSE curriculum consists of the following courses:
* CS1401: High Performance Computing (3-0-0, 3 credits)
* CS14XX: Professional Elective-IV (3-0-0, 3 credits)
* CS14XX: Professional Elective-V (3-0-0, 3 credits)
* Open Elective - I (2-0-0, 2 credits)
* CS1403: High Performance Computing Laboratory (0-1-2, 2 credits)
* CS1489: Seminar and Technical Writing (0-0-0, 2 credits)
* CS1495: Minor Project (0-0-0, 2 credits)
* CS1491: Short Term Industrial/EPICS/Research Experience (0-0-0, 2 credits)

These courses prepare students for the 8th semester by providing them with a strong foundation in high-performance computing, professional electives, and laboratory experience. The minor project and short-term industrial/research experience also help students develop practical skills and apply theoretical knowledge to real-world problems. The seminar and technical writing course enable students to improve their communication and writing skills, which are essential for the major project in the 8th semester.

In the 8th semester, students will work on their major project (CS1498), which requires them to identify a domain-specific topic, review literature, develop a prototype/model, and analyze results. The courses in the 7th semester, particularly the professional electives, minor project, and seminar, help students develop the necessary skills and knowledge to tackle the major project in the 8th semester.

(Source: 1_B.Tech_Computer_Science_and_Engineering-Curriculum-new-2024-25_2024-7-19-15-40-32)

Total: 19 credits. The seminar, minor project, and industry experience prepare students for the 8th semester which is heavily project-focused (Major Project worth 6 credits) with only elective coursework.
